text: The surgeon begins the operation by mobilizing the liver, using a robotic hook and bipolar graspers to take down the left coronary and triangular ligaments. The falciform ligament is dissected up to the hepatocarpal confluence, with care taken to avoid injuring the left hepatic vein. The left lateral section of the liver is elevated superiorly, and adhesions on the inferior surface are sharply dissected. The robotic arm with a biograsper aids in the dissection. The orenseous ligament is opened along its trajectory, and the gastrohepatic ligament is opened to check for an accessory left hepatic artery. The liver is elevated cephalad, and adhesions to the falciform ligament are removed with the hook artery.

The surgeon biopsies a small subcapsular lesion using energized scissors, sending the sample for frozen section analysis. An intraoperative liver ultrasound confirms the lesion's presence in segment three of the liver. The base of the umbilical vein is exposed using lateral traction on the future remnant and medial traction on the left lateral section, preparing for inflow vascular control to minimize bleeding.

The surgeon uses a hook cautery to dissect the left portal vein branches supplying segments two and three of the liver. Inflow vascular control is achieved with straight vascular bulldog clamps placed on the left portal vein and left hepatic artery branches. The liver parenchyma is then superficially transected using monopolar scissors cautery, while the assistant uses laparoscopic suction to evacuate smoke and blood from the liver surface.

The surgeon uses an extended vessel sealer to carefully divide the deeper liver parenchyma, paying close attention to avoid injuring crossing vascular branches. The laparoscopic linear vascular stapler is then used to transect the portal pedicle going to segments two and three, including the baldach, in an en bloc fashion. The stapling is performed along the orensis ligament, heading toward the stump of the left hepatic vein, which is clearly visualized with minimal bleeding observed during the transection.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the superficial liver parenchyma, exposing the stump of the left hepatic vein. A second linear vascular stapler is fired to transect the left hepatic vein flush to the inferior vena cava along the ligament. The procedure is completed without significant bleeding. A third stapler is used to finish the liver resection, achieving a bloodless outcome. Hemostasis of the superficial liver parenchyma is achieved with a bipolar robotic instrument. A large endoscopic retrieval bag is inserted to remove the specimen, and the liver resection surface appears dry without bleeding or bile leak.
